# -*- sh -*-
# See http://rigaux.org/language-study/diagram.html for the format of this file
#claude: auto-generated by Claude Code (inspired from my other lineage.txt)

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Emulator / instruction set simulator lineage
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

#src: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Instruction_set_simulator
#src: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emulator
#src: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_System/360
#src: wikipedia articles on different emulators

# This covers the lineage of instruction set simulators, CPU emulators,
# and binary translators — the category that Plan 9's 5i belongs to.

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pre-Historical
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

# IBM coined the term "emulator" for System/360 compatibility mode:
# microcode that executed 1401 instructions directly on 360 hardware.

*IBM 360 emulation
  1964
  SY:6
  # first use of the term "emulator"
  # microcode-based compatibility with 1401/1440/7090

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Educational / Academic
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*MIX
  1968
  SY:2
  # Knuth's TAOCP includes a MIX simulator
  # hypothetical machine for teaching algorithms

*MMIX
  1999
  MIX
  # Knuth's modernized replacement for MIX
  # RISC architecture, 64-bit

*SPIM
  1990
  SY:2
  # James Larus, U. Wisconsin; MIPS R2000/R3000 simulator
  # widely used in computer architecture courses

SimpleScalar
  1997
  # U. Wisconsin; cycle-accurate microarchitecture simulator
  # for computer architecture research

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Research
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Shade
  1993
  # Sun Microsystems Labs; fast instruction-set simulator
  # dynamic binary translation for SPARC/MIPS

*SimOS
  1995
  Shade
  # Stanford, Mendel Rosenblum; full system MIPS simulator
  # predecessor to Disco and VMware

Disco
  1997
  SimOS
  # Stanford; virtual machine monitor for MIPS/IRIX
  # directly led to founding of VMware

Simics
  1998
  SimOS
  SY:6
  # SICS (Sweden), then Virtutech, now Intel/Wind River
  # first to boot unmodified Solaris in simulation (1997)

HP Dynamo
  2000
  # HP Labs; dynamic binary optimizer for PA-RISC
  # pioneered dynamic optimization techniques

DynamoRIO
  2002
  HP Dynamo
  # HP + MIT; dynamic binary instrumentation for x86
  # open-sourced in 2009

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Plan 9
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Plan 9 5i
  1993
  SY:1
  # ARM instruction set simulator
  # interprets 5.out binaries, syscall emulation on host

Plan 9 ki
  1993
  SY:1
  # SPARC instruction set simulator

Plan 9 vi
  1993
  SY:1
  # MIPS instruction set simulator

Plan 9 qi
  1993
  SY:1
  # POWER instruction set simulator

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Open Source
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*SimH
  1993
  SY:2
  # Bob Supnik (DEC); simulators for historic computers
  # PDP-11, VAX, IBM 1401, and many others

*Bochs
  1994
  SY:2
  # Kevin Lawton; x86 PC emulator, interpretive
  # used for OS development (e.g., early Linux kernel debugging)

NESticle
  1997
  SY:2
  # Bloodlust Software; first popular freeware NES emulator
  # iconic in the 90s console emulation scene

Snes9x
  1997
  SY:2
  # Gary Henderson + Jeremy Koot; merger of Snes96 and Snes97
  # one of the best SNES emulators, still maintained

*MAME
  1997
  SY:2
  # Nicola Salmoria; arcade machine emulator
  # preservation-oriented, thousands of machines

*DOSBox
  2002
  Bochs
  SY:2
  # x86 + DOS emulator for running old PC games

*Wine
  1993
  SY:2
  # user-level emulator for Windows API on Unix
  # syscall-boundary translation, not CPU emulation

*Valgrind
  2002
  SY:2
  # Julian Seward; dynamic binary instrumentation
  # memcheck, cachegrind; x86 shadow execution

*QEMU
  2003
  Shade
  SY:2
  # Fabrice Bellard; fast full-system and user-mode emulator
  # dynamic binary translation, many target architectures
QEMU 0.9
  2007
  # KVM support; KVM merged into Linux 2.6.20 same year
QEMU 0.10
  2008
  # TCG (Tiny Code Generator) replaces dyngen
  # portable backend, no longer depends on GCC 3.x internals
*QEMU 1.0
  2011
  # first stable release; ~20000 commits from ~400 contributors
*QEMU 2.0
  2014
*QEMU 5.0
  2020

*TinyEMU
  2017
  QEMU
  SY:2
  # Fabrice Bellard; small RISC-V and x86 emulator
  # written from scratch, much simpler than QEMU

*JSLinux
  2011
  QEMU
  SY:2
  # Fabrice Bellard; Linux running in a web browser
  # x86 emulator written in JavaScript

*JSLinux 2
  2018
  TinyEMU
  SY:2
  # rewritten using TinyEMU compiled to WebAssembly
  # supports RISC-V and x86, much faster than JS version

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Binary Translation (Industry)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*FX!32
  1996
  SY:6
  # DEC; x86 to Alpha binary translator for Windows NT
  # profile-directed translation, pioneered hybrid approach

*Transmeta Crusoe
  2000
  SY:6
  # hardware + code morphing software; x86 via VLIW translation
  # Linus Torvalds worked there

*Rosetta
  2006
  FX!32
  SY:6
  # Apple; PowerPC to x86 translation (Mac transition)
  # based on Transitive QuickTransit technology

*Rosetta 2
  2020
  Rosetta
  SY:6
  # Apple; x86-64 to ARM64 translation (Apple Silicon transition)
  # ahead-of-time + JIT translation

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Industry (Virtualization, not really emulators)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*VMware
  1999
  Disco
  SY:6
  # founded by Rosenblum, Greene, Bugnion, Devine, Wang
  # first commercial x86 virtualization

VirtualPC
  1997
  SY:6
  # Connectix, then Microsoft; x86 emulation on Mac
  # later became Hyper-V lineage

Xen
  2003
  SY:2
  # U. Cambridge; paravirtualization hypervisor
  # used by Amazon EC2 initially

Parallels
  2006
  SY:6
  # first mainstream virtualization for Intel Macs

KVM
  2007
  QEMU
  SY:2
  # Avi Kivity; merged into Linux 2.6.20
  # hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x / AMD-V)

VirtualBox
  2007
  SY:2
  # Innotek, then Sun, then Oracle
  # open source hosted hypervisor for x86/ARM

Hercules
  1999
  IBM 360 emulation
  SY:2
  # Roger Bowler; open source IBM mainframe emulator
  # System/370, ESA/390, z/Architecture
